segments.s087-g25: The phrase 'از ایران سپه' uses the preposition از with a genitive-like meaning ('the army of Iran' / 'the Iranian army'); adjective/adverb 'بیشتر' follows the noun and is comparative ('more'). The verb شد is simple past (here with the sense 'became/got'), so the whole clause reads literally 'the army of Iran became more tired.'
segments.s087-g26: وزان = و + زان, an adverbial 'and/from that time' (here 'from then on'). 'روی پیکار' is a poetic collocation: روی ('face/turn') + پیکار ('battle') = 'the turn/face toward battle' — i.e. engagement in fighting. پیوسته شد is past-become ('became continuous' or 'became joined/constant'). Together: 'and from that time the engagement in battle became continuous.'
segments.s087-g27: به here marks direction/state ('into / in'), so 'به بیچارگی' = 'in/with helplessness/misery.' 'روی برگاشتند' is a standard compound: روی (face) + برگشتند (they turned back/returned) meaning 'they turned away/returned' — برگاشتند is 3rd-person plural past.
segments.s087-g28: به هامون indicates location/direction ('to/onto the Hamun plain'). برافگنده is a participial/adjectival form ('spread out, flung down') describing what was left. بگذاشتند is past plural 'they placed/left' (here 'they left [them]'), so the clause means 'they left [them] spread upon the Hamun.'
segments.s087-g29: The nominal phrase 'دل نوذر' ('Nowzar's heart') uses juxtaposition with the proper name; the first از ('از غم') expresses cause/source ('from/because of sorrow'). 'پر' here is the adjective 'full (of)' and is followed by another از ('پر از درد' = 'full of pain'). بود is the copular past 'was.' The clause literally: 'Nowzar's heart was full of pain because of sorrow.'
segments.s087-g30: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/that'). تاجش contains the possessive enclitic -ش ('his crown'). ز is the poetic form of از ('from'), so 'ز اختر' = 'from the star(s)'; پر از گرد = 'full of dust' (گرد = dust). بود is again past 'was.' The clause explains why his heart was sorrowful: 'for his crown was full of dust from the stars.'
segments.s087-g31: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' از دشت = 'from the plain.' بنشست is a literary/past form used with sounds and happenings ('sat'/'rose'/'sounded' in poetic usage), so آوای کوس بنشست = 'the sound of the drum arose/sounded.' Word order places the temporal clause first: 'When the drum sounded from the plain...'.
segments.s087-g32: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past 'he commanded/ordered.' تا introduces the subordinate clause of command ('that / so that'), followed by پیش او رفت طوس — 'طوس' (Tous) is the subject of رفت (went); پیش او = 'before him / in his presence.' So the clause means 'he commanded that Tous go before him.'
segments.s087-g17: سرانجام is an adverb meaning “at last/finally” placed at the clause start. نوذر is the proper name (Nowzar) as subject. ز + قلب سپاه is a prepositional phrase: ز (poetic = from) governs قلب سپاه where قلب (literally “heart”) means “center/core” and سپاه is “army/host”; word order is noun + modifier (قلبِ سپاه understood as “the army’s center”).
segments.s087-g18: بیامد is simple past third-person singular “he came.” به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase meaning “to/near”; in classical prose/poetry به + noun often marks approach. او is the third‑person singular pronoun “him.” رزمخواه is an adjectival/epithetic noun (literally “war‑seeker” / “eager for battle”) describing the subject; here it functions as an appositive describing نوذر’s disposition as he approached.
segments.s087-g19: چنان is an adverb “thus/so” modifying the action. نیزه ... انداختند: two identical nouns (نیزه = 'spear') frame the preposition بر, here meaning “against” or “upon”; انداختند is past plural “they threw/ hurled” (verb stem انداز + past plural suffix -ند), so the clause says they hurled spears at spears.
segments.s087-g20: سنان here refers to spear‑points or spear‑tips (plural notion); یک به دیگر is the idiom “one to/at the other” (به = toward), and برافراختند is past plural “they raised/erected” (برافراشتن) describing the action of holding/raising spear‑tips toward each other. The clause is parallel to the first, describing mutual thrusting/raising.
segments.s087-g21: که introduces a subordinate clause here with a sense of result/remark (“so that / that”); بر هم نپیچد is a negative present/subjunctive form meaning “does not / would not twist/coiI upon one another” (ن‑ negative prefix + پیچد subjunctive/present). بران گونه is a poetic inversion: بران = “upon that/thereon,” گونه = “manner/way”; together بران گونه مار = “a snake in that manner” (word order is inverted for poetic effect).
segments.s087-g22: شهان را: شهان (plural/poetic for kings) with را marking them as the topical/affected party. چنین is “thus/so.” کی here functions rhetorically as “when/ever” (classical usage) and بود is past “was”; کارزار means “battle.” The clause is a rhetorical question: “When has there ever been a battle like this for kings?”
segments.s087-g23: چنین تا introduces duration “thus/until”; تا شب تیره آمد = “until the dark night came” (تا = until, شب تیره = night‑dark/ominous night). به تنگ is an idiomatic phrase meaning “into a narrow/pressed condition” or “to be put to the straits” (به + تنگ), describing their situation as constrained; classical Persian often uses spatial adjectives metaphorically.
segments.s087-g24: برو here functions as a narrative connective in poetry meaning roughly “then/thereupon” (not the modern imperative sense), signaling the next event. خیره شد is a compound meaning “became stunned/was transfixed” (خیره = staring/fixed; شد = became). دست پور پشنگ is a possessive NP in inverted order: دست (hand) + پور پشنگ (the son/descendant named Pashang), i.e. “the hand of Pour‑Pashang.” Grammatically, the clause says that Pour‑Pashang’s hand became motionless/stunned.
segments.s087-g1: برآسود is a past, third-person singular verb meaning “was at rest/ceased.” پس is an adverb meaning “then” and modifies the whole clause. لشکر is the noun “host/army”; the prepositional phrase از هر دو روی (“from/out of both sides”) follows it, where روی here means “side.” The phrase order is poetic but functions as: “Then the army, from both sides, was at rest.”
segments.s087-g2: برفتند is past, third-person plural “they went/departed.” روز دوم is a time adverbial “on the second day.” جنگجوی here names the agents (“the warriors”); in poetic word order the verb appears before the time phrase and the agent follows, so the natural reading is “On the second day the warriors departed.”
segments.s087-g3: رده is the noun “row/line/rank” and is the object here. برکشیدند is a past, third-person plural transitive verb “they raised/drew up.” ایرانیان is the subject “the Iranians,” placed after the verb in this poetic order (verb–object–subject reading). Together: “The Iranians drew up the ranks.”
segments.s087-g4: چنان چون (lit. “such as/just as”) introduces a comparison. بود is the past copula “was.” ساز here means “arrangement/device/means” (i.e., the battle arrangement), and جنگ qualifies that as a war/battle arrangement; کیان is the possessor “the Kians/the kings.” Word order is noun + modifier + possessor: “just as was the Kians’ war-arrangement.”
segments.s087-g5: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” افراسیاب is the proper-name subject. آن سپه را is the definite object (“that host”) with را marking the definite direct object. بدید is past, third-person singular “he saw.” The structure is “When Afrasiab saw that host.”
segments.s087-g6: بزد is past, third-sing. “he struck.” کوس رویین is a noun phrase “the iron/bronze drum,” where رویین is an adjective following the noun (typical in Persian poetry). و links the two verbs; صف برکشید is a parallel past verb phrase “he raised/drew up the battle line,” with برکشید as the past transitive verb. Both verbs describe Afrasiab’s actions in sequence.
segments.s087-g7: چنان شد is an impersonal/neutral past phrase “it became so” or “thus it was.” ز گرد is a prepositional phrase meaning “from the dust,” where گرد here means “dust” (the dust raised by riding). سواران جهان is a genitive phrase “the horsemen of the world” (سواران = horsemen, جهان = the world); together: “Thus it happened from the dust of the world’s horsemen.”
segments.s087-g8: که introduces the result clause “that …”. خورشید is the subject “the sun.” گفتی is an archaic/poetic particle (variant of گویی) meaning “as if/it seemed” and functions to introduce a comparison or seeming; this sense is not among the provided sense options. شد here is the copular/past auxiliary “was/appeared,” and اندر نهان means “in hiding/hidden within.” The clause reads “that the sun, as if, was hidden within.”
segments.s087-g57: ندانم is the fused negative + present form meaning 'I do not know' (na‑ + دانم). که introduces a subordinate clause here with a sense like 'whether/that'. باشد is the optative/subjunctive third‑person form of بودن used to express possibility ('may be' / 'will be'). جزین = 'except this' (poetic order: جز + این). The clause literally reads 'I don't know that/if a meeting may be except this.'
segments.s087-g58: یک امشب places a limiting adverbial 'one/once tonight' (یک here = 'for once' / 'one time' modifying امشب). بکوشیم is the 1st‑plur. subjunctive/hortatory of کوشدن (ب‑ prefix + کوشیم) meaning 'let us try' or 'we should try'. دست پسین is the direct object: دست = 'hand/effort' used metaphorically and پسین = 'later/second/rear', so 'try the later hand' (i.e. make a last/alternative attempt tonight).
segments.s087-g59: شب و روز is an adverbial phrase 'night and day' placed up front. دارید is 2nd‑plur. present 'you have' / 'maintain' (addressing the addressees). کارآگهان is a plural/collective noun 'the informed/knowing ones' (agents or informers) functioning as the object: 'keep informers night and day.'
segments.s087-g60: بجویید is the 2nd‑plur. imperative/subjunctive of جستن 'seek' (a polite/command form). هشیار functions as an adjective 'alert/shrewd' modifying the noun phrase هشیار کار جهان: کار جهان 'the affairs of the world' (کار here = 'affair'), so the whole phrase means 'seek alert men for the affairs of the world' or 'find vigilant agents to watch world affairs.'
segments.s087-g61: ازین is the poetic/compacted from of از + این 'from this'. ار is the conditional 'if'. دهند is the 3rd‑plur. present subjunctive/indicative of دادن 'they give'. بد here qualifies آگهی: 'bad/unfavourable information' or simply 'information' with negative sense. The sequence means 'if (any) from this army give information/notices (to the enemy)'.
segments.s087-g62: شود is an impersonal/3rd‑person future/optative of شدن 'will become' or 'will be'. تیره is a predicate adjective 'dark/ominous' (here 'dimmed' or 'tarnished'). این فر شاهنشهی = 'this (royal) glory of kingship' (فر = royal glory, شاهنشهی = imperial/kingship). Word order places the verb first in customary poetic style: 'it will become dark the glory of this kingship.'
segments.s087-g63: شما is the addressee 'you' (plural/polite). دل مدارید is an exhortation built with مدارید (imperative/optative of مداریدن) meaning 'be gentle/compassionate to (the) heart(s)' or more idiomatically 'soften your hearts / show mercy'. بس here functions as an intensifier 'very/exceedingly' (classical use), and مستمند is the noun 'needy/poor' as the object: 'be merciful to the very needy.'
segments.s087-g64: که introduces the reason/explanation 'for/because'. باید is modal 'must/it is necessary'. چنین بد = 'thus/badly' (چنین 'thus' + بد 'bad/ill'). ز چرخ بلند is a poetic ablative 'from the high wheel/sky' where چرخ often means 'fate/sky' and بلند = 'lofty/high' — together 'by the high heavens/fate.' The clause gives the justification: 'for it must be thus, ill from the high heavens (i.e. by fate).'
segments.s087-g65: The clause is a compact, slightly poetic statement where 'یکی را' ('someone') is marked with the object marker را for emphasis. The verb phrase is 'به جنگ اندر آید زمان' — literally 'time comes into/into war' — here best understood as a causative/impersonal temporal expression: 'time (or the occasion) leads someone into battle.' Word order puts the temporal noun زمان after the verb for emphasis; آید is present/factual 'comes' (classical present/future sense).
segments.s087-g66: This is a verbless nominal clause parallel to the previous one: 'یکی' ('another person') + circumstantial 'با کلاه مهی' ('with a moon‑like helmet') followed by the predicate adjective 'شادمان' ('joyful'). The copula 'است' is omitted (a common poetic ellipsis), so read as 'one is joyful with a moon‑like helmet.' 'مهی' is an adjectival/epithet form 'moon‑like/beautiful' modifying کلاه.
segments.s087-g67: 'تن کشته' is a determiner + past participle phrase 'the body of the slain' (or 'the slain body'). 'با' introduces the comparison 'with a corpse' (مرده). The predicate 'یکسان شود' — adjective 'یکسان' + شود ('becomes') — expresses 'becomes identical/equal to.' The verb شود is the classical present/future auxiliary meaning 'becomes/is made.'
segments.s087-g68: Two short clauses: 'تپد یک زمان' — intransitive present 'it throbs/palpitates' (tپد) with 'یک زمان' as a temporal adverbial 'for a moment/for a time.' Then 'بازش آسان شود' — 'باز' ('again') cliticized with '-ش' (third‑person clitic 'it/him') and 'آسان شود' ('becomes easy/calm'); together 'then it becomes calm again.' The verb شود again expresses change of state.
segments.s087-g69: Syntactically this reads as '[Proper name] Maran gave him counsels' followed by a temporal clause 'چون سزید' ('when/as it was deserved'). Here 'مران' behaves as the agent (proper name), 'پندها' is the direct object 'admonitions/advice', and 'بدادش' functions as a past action with the enclitic '-ش' ('to him'). Note: the lemma senses provided for بدادش do not match the verbal usage here, so that lemma's sense is uncertain in the supplied list.
segments.s087-g70: 'پس' is the conjunctive adverb 'then/thereupon.' 'آن دست شاهانه' is a noun phrase 'that royal/regal hand' (شاهانه as an adjectival epithet). The verb 'بیرون کشید' is a compound past 'drew out/withdrew' (بیرون + کشید); poetically the object marker را is omitted and the verb follows the noun phrase. The listed senses for کشید in the data do not include 'to draw/pull,' so the correct verbal sense is not in the provided list.
segments.s087-g71: 'گرفت' is simple past 'he took/seized.' The object 'آن دو فرزند را' ('those two children') is marked by را as definite/accusative. The prepositional phrase 'در کنار' ('to the side' / 'aside') indicates the direction/placement. Word order places the verb before the object+prepositional phrase, a typical flexible poetic order.
segments.s087-g72: فرو + ریخت together function as a compound verb meaning 'poured down' or 'fell down.' 'آب' is the direct object ('water' — here tears in context). 'از مژه شهریار' is a genitive/agentive phrase 'from the king's eyelash/eyelid' where از marks source. The construction describes tears pouring from the ruler's eye.
segments.s087-g33: بشد is an archaic/pre-Classical past-form verb placed at the start for narrative emphasis meaning 'they came/it happened'. The following coordinated subject طوس و گستهم names the two men; با او 'with him' marks accompaniment, and به هم is a short adverbial phrase 'together' (به + هم). Word order (verb before subject) is a poetic inversion common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s087-g34: This is a parallel descriptive phrase: 'لبان پر ز باد' and 'روان پر ز غم' (= 'lips full of wind' / 'soul full of grief'). پر ز is a poetic way to say 'full of' (پر + ز ≈ 'filled from/of'), ز is the poetic variant of از, and روان here means 'spirit/soul'. The two halves are linked by و 'and' to paint an external (lips) and internal (spirit) condition.
segments.s087-g35: بگفت is the simple past 'he said'; آنک is an archaic/poetic introducer ('that/which' or 'the one who') used to lead into the content clause. The embedded clause 'در دل مرا درد چیست' literally follows Persian order 'in heart my pain what-is' (چیست = copula 'is' at sentence end); مرا (here segmented) marks possession 'my'. The whole is the reported question 'what pain is in my heart?'.
segments.s087-g36: همی is the continuous/iterative marker 'kept/continued to'; گفت here implies he kept speaking (poetic economy—گفت used as 'spoke'). چندی و چندی is a reduplicated adverbial idiom 'for a while' (repetition emphasizes duration). گریست is past 'he wept', so the clause says he repeatedly spoke and for a time wept.
segments.s087-g37: The verb یاد کرد ('recalled', lit. 'made remembrance') governs the prepositional phrase از اندرز 'from the counsel/advice'. فرخ پدر is a genitival/designation attached to اندرز (here a proper name or epithet + 'father'), so the phrase means 'he remembered the advice of Farakh, [his] father.' Word order is object/prepositional phrase before the verb, typical in poetry.
segments.s087-g38: This is again two parallel nominal phrases describing bodily states: 'پر از خون جگر' = 'filled with (blood of) jigar' (jigar here is poetic for the seat of feeling), then 'لب پر از باد سرد' = 'lip(s) full of cold wind'. پر از is the adjectival pattern 'full of X', and the phrase juxtaposes inner wound (blood/heart) and outer exposure (cold wind).
segments.s087-g39: کجا at the start is a rhetorical 'where/when' (where had he been told...). گفته بودش is a pluperfect/passive-periphrastic sense 'had been told to him' (گفته + بودش), and که introduces the content clause 'that from the Turks and China...'. The sequence places the source ('از ترک و چین') after که as part of the reported information.
segments.s087-g40: سپاهی is an indefinite/collective noun 'an army' or 'a force'; بیاید is the present/subjunctive/optative form 'may come' or 'should come' used in indirect speech after گفتن/گفته بود; به ایران‌زمین gives the destination 'into the land of Iran'. The clause expresses reported fear or prediction that a host would come to Iran.
segments.s087-g9: دهاده (an emphatic/collective noun) appears first as the subject meaning a great number or multitude; برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'issued forth/rose'. ز هر دو گروه is a prepositional phrase 'from both companies/troops' (ز + هر دو گروه) that follows the verb and specifies the source. The word order Subject – Verb – prepositional source is typical of the epic, and برآمد is a simple past 3rd-plural sense applied to the multitudinous subject.
segments.s087-g10: بیابان is the topical noun 'open country/plain' and is followed by the negated copula نبود 'there was not/was no'. ایچ (an indefinite/negative particle) + پیدا 'visible/seen' form a verbless predicate 'nothing visible', and ز کوه is the source/modifier 'from the mountain'. The clause thus literally says 'There was no open country — nothing visible from the mountain.'
segments.s087-g11: سپَه is the subject 'the host/army' and آویختند is the past 3rd‑plural 'they clung/attached (to one another)'. بر هم is a reciprocal phrase 'to one another / upon each other'. برانسان here is a descriptive/nominal element (literally 'burnouss/cloaks' or 'on men') attached to the scene — poetically implying the men and their cloaks in the mêlée — and sits at the front for emphasis. The verb is a simple past describing how the host closed in.
segments.s087-g12: چو introduces a simile 'as/like', رود روان is the image 'a flowing river' (رود = torrent/river, روان = flowing). خون همی ریختند is the main clause: خون 'blood' (object/theme) + همی (an older progressive particle) + ریختند (past of ریختن 'to pour/spill'): together 'blood was poured/was spilling'. The phrase thus reads 'blood poured like a flowing river.'
segments.s087-g13: به هر سو که is a locative frame 'to/at every side that/where'. قارن here is a proper name (Qaren) used appositionally and followed by شدی — an archaic past form of شدن/شد 'became/turned/was' — and رزمخواه is an epithet 'the war‑seeking one' (appositive to قارن). Grammatically this is a relative/temporal clause 'wherever Qaren (the fighter) went/was', with که introducing the subordinate clause.
segments.s087-g14: فرو is an adverb 'downward/away', ریختی is the past 'poured' (ریختن), and خون is the theme 'blood'. ز + گرد سیاه is a prepositional source phrase 'from the black گرد'; here گرد functions poetically as a mass/raised cloud (i.e. dust or a dense black host/cloud) and سیاه modifies it 'black'. The clause literally says 'blood poured down from the black cloud/host.'
segments.s087-g15: کجا is an interrogative/locative 'where/wherever' placed frontally; خاستی is a past verb 'rose/appeared' (classical past). گرد افراسیاب is a genitival phrase 'the host/assembly of Afrasiab' (گرد used poetically for a gathered mass/host). The clause is thus 'where did Afrasiab's host rise?' or more idiomatically 'wherever Afrasiab's host rose.'
segments.s087-g16: همه is an intensifier 'all/entirely'; خون شدی is a past construction 'became blood' (شدی = شد/wascome) with دشت 'the plain' as the affected noun (word order is inverted for poetic effect: 'the plain became all blood'). چون رود آب is a simile 'like a river of water' (چون = like, رود = river, آب = water). The whole says that the plain was wholly turned into blood, like a flowing river.
segments.s087-g41: ازیشان (az-īshān) is a fused prepositional phrase meaning 'because of them' and introduces the cause. ترا is the enclitic object 'to you' (ت = 2nd-person pronoun) with را as the object/recipient marker; together they indicate whose heart is affected. دل شود دردمند is a predicative clause: دل 'heart' with شود (3rd-person singular of شدن, 'become') as the copular verb and دردمند as the resulting adjective 'in pain/sorrowful' — literally 'a heart becomes sorrowful (for you)'.
segments.s087-g42: بسی is an adverbial intensifier ('much/very') placed before the prepositional phrase. بر سپاه تو is the target phrase ('upon/against your army') with بر as the preposition and تو as the possessor following the noun (classical Persian word order). آید (3rd-person singular of آمدن) is the verb 'comes' and گزند (lit. 'harm') is the thing that 'comes' — the clause reads literally 'much upon your army comes harm.'
segments.s087-g43: ز گفتار شاه is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'from the speech/words of the king' (ز = 'from'). آمد is the verb 'came/appeared' and اکنون 'now' is an adverb; نشان ('sign/token') is the noun that appears. Word order here is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: prepositional phrase + verb + adverb + noun = 'from the king's words now came a sign.'
segments.s087-g44: فراز is an adverb/prepositional adverb meaning 'on high / to the heights'. آمد is again the verb 'came/ascended' (3rd-person sing.). آن روز 'that day' locates the action in time, and گردنکشان ('the proud ones' / 'rebels') is the plural agent that performed the action. The clause reads 'to the heights came that day the proud ones.'
segments.s087-g45: کس here functions as an indefinite negative 'no one'. از نامه نامداران is a prepositional phrase 'in/from the records of the renowned' (نامه = record/book, نامداران = 'the famed/illustrious'). نخواند is the negative past/presentive form of خواندن used here with the sense 'did not write/record/read out' — together: 'no one in the records of the famed wrote/recorded'.
segments.s087-g46: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/ how'). چندین سپه is a quantified NP 'many a host / several armies'. کس is an indefinite subject 'one/anyone' and ز ترکان is the source phrase 'from the Turks' (ز = 'from'). براند (3rd-person singular of راندن) means 'drove away / put to flight'. Word order places the quantified object before the subject, with the verb at the end as typical in classical poetic Persian.
segments.s087-g47: شما را places 'you' as the affected/indirect object (شما with the object marker را). سوی پارس is the direction 'toward Pars (Persia)'. باید شدن is a periphrasis: باید ('it is necessary / must') plus شدن ('to become' / infinitive) yields an obligation 'you must go / it is necessary that you go' in this classical usage — i.e. 'you must set out toward Pars.'
segments.s087-g48: This segment supplies the infinitival complements of the obligation: شبستان ('bedchamber, inner chamber') is the object to be fetched; بیاوردن and آمدن are infinitives 'to bring' and 'to come/return'. The coordination with و links the two actions expected under the preceding باید شدن ('you must ... to bring the bedchamber and to come').
segments.s087-g49: وزان جا is a fused, archaic prepositional phrase (وز + آن + جا) meaning 'from that place'; it sets the scene. کشیدن here is the narrative verb 'to march/advance' (infinitive form used in epic narrative) and سوی + noun marks the direction ('toward Mount Zaveh'). Word order: prepositional phrase + verb + directional phrase.
segments.s087-g50: بران is an archaic compound (بر + آن) meaning 'thereon/thence'; it points to the next locale. کوهِ البرز names the destination. بردن is the verb 'to take/lead' used in narrative (past sense 'led/took'), and گروه is the object 'a band/troop'. The clause thus means 'thence they led a troop to Mount Alborz.'
segments.s087-g51: ازیدر is an uncertain/archaic form (printed here as given) that functions like a locative/adverbial 'from here' (see note). کنون is the time adverb 'now'. زی is a classical preposition 'from/of' introducing the following noun; سپاهان is the noun 'armies' and روید is the narrative intransitive verb 'appear/show themselves'. Order is adverbial + locative + subject + verb.
segments.s087-g52: وزین is an attributive adjective 'stately/weighty' modifying لشکر 'host/army'. خوی + ش is the older split form of خویش 'his/own' (خوی = 'kin/own', ش = enclitic 'his'). پنهان is an adjective/participle 'hidden', and روید (here) functions as the resultive/inchoative verb 'became/kept (so)'—together 'his stately army kept itself concealed.'
segments.s087-g53: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/on account of'; کار شما is the noun phrase 'your deed(s)/action(s)'. دل‌شکسته is a compound adjective 'broken‑hearted', and شوند (third plural subjunctive/epic resultive) expresses the resulting state 'they become/are made'. The clause literally: 'From your deed(s), they became broken‑hearted.'
segments.s087-g54: برین is a fused form (بر + این) meaning 'because of this / on this account'. خستگی 'fatigue' is the cause-noun; نیز 'also' links the clause to the previous one. خسته شوند uses خسته (tired) plus شوند (become) to mean 'they too became tired/exhausted.' Word order: causal phrase + noun + connector + predicate.
segments.s087-g55: ز تخمِ فریدون is a possessive/genitive phrase 'from the seed/offspring of Fereydun' (تخم = 'seed/lineage' used for 'offspring'). مگر here functions as 'except' (classical usage), and یک دو تن is a fixed idiom 'one or two persons' (تن = 'person'). Together: 'From Fereydun's line, except for one or two persons...'.
segments.s087-g56: برد is the narrative past of بردن 'to take' (here used transitively as 'took/killed'); جان is 'life' (object). ازین (از + این) means 'from this', with بی‌شمار 'countless' modifying انجمن 'assembly/host'. Grammatically: برد (verb) + جان (object) + ازین بی‌شمار انجمن (from this countless assembly) = 'they took the lives of this countless assembly.' Note: the provided lemma senses for برد did not list the verbal past use, so the sense index below is -1.

sentences.s087-s13: The Iranian army grew more weary; from that time the fighting became continuous.
sentences.s087-s14: They turned back in misery; they left (them) spread upon the Hamun.
sentences.s087-s15: Nowzar's heart was full of grief and pain, for his crown was full of dust from the stars.
sentences.s087-s16: When the sound of the war-drum rose from the plain, he ordered that Tous go before him.
sentences.s087-s9: At last Nowzar came out from the heart of the host and drew near him, eager for battle.
sentences.s087-s10: Thus they cast spear against spear, and they lifted the spear‑points toward one another.
sentences.s087-s11: So that a serpent would not coil against itself in that fashion — when has there ever been such a battle for kings?
sentences.s087-s12: So it went on until the dark night came and they were pressed into a narrow place; then the hand of Pour‑Pashang became motionless/stunned.
sentences.s087-s1: Then the hosts on both sides were at rest; on the second day the warriors departed.
sentences.s087-s2: The Iranians drew up their ranks — just as was the Kians’ battle-arrangement.
sentences.s087-s3: When Afrasiab saw that host, he struck the brazen drum and drew up his battle line.
sentences.s087-s4: Thus it became, from the dust of the world’s horsemen, that the sun seemed to be hidden.
sentences.s087-s29: I do not know whether there will be any meeting besides this; tonight, once, let us try the later hand.
sentences.s087-s30: Have (informers) night and day; seek alert men for the affairs of the world.
sentences.s087-s31: If from this army they give information, the splendour of this imperial kingship will be darkened.
sentences.s087-s32: Do not harden your hearts, you — be merciful to the very needy; for it must be so, ill by the high heavens.
sentences.s087-s33: At one time time drives someone into battle; at another, someone with a moon‑like helmet is joyful.
sentences.s087-s34: The slain body becomes the same as a corpse; it throbs for a while, then grows calm again.
sentences.s087-s35: Maran gave him counsels; when it was deserved, then that royal hand drew forth.
sentences.s087-s36: He took those two children aside; water poured down from the king's eyelash.
sentences.s087-s17: Tūs and Gostaham came up to him together — their lips full of wind and their spirits full of sorrow.
sentences.s087-s18: He said, 'What is the pain in my heart?' — he kept speaking and for a while wept.
sentences.s087-s19: He recalled the counsel of fair Farakh, his father — his heart full of blood, his lips full of cold wind.
sentences.s087-s20: Where had he been told that an army from the Turks and China would come into the land of Iran?
sentences.s087-s5: Multitudes issued forth from both companies; from the mountain not a single stretch of plain was visible.
sentences.s087-s6: The host grappled together, and blood poured like a flowing river.
sentences.s087-s7: On every side where Qaren, the war‑seeker, turned, blood poured down from the black cloud.
sentences.s087-s8: Wherever Afrasiab's host rose, the plain became entirely blood, like a river of water.
sentences.s087-s21: Because of them your heart will become sorrowful; great harm will come upon your army.
sentences.s087-s22: From the king's words a sign now appeared; that day the proud ones ascended to the heights.
sentences.s087-s23: No one among the renowned wrote in the records how many hosts anyone had driven from the Turks.
sentences.s087-s24: You must go toward Pars — to bring the bedchamber and to come (back).
sentences.s087-s25: From that place they marched toward Mount Zaveh, and thence led a troop to the Alborz mountain.
sentences.s087-s26: Now from here the armies showed themselves, and his stately host remained hidden.
sentences.s087-s27: Because of your deeds they became broken‑hearted; from that weariness they too were exhausted.
sentences.s087-s28: From the seed of Fereydun, except for one or two men, they took the lives of this countless assembly.
